findings.title: statSync on source file blocks event loop on every segment request
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `statSync(sourcePath)` with `await stat(sourcePath)` (from `fs/promises`). The stat result is only needed to compute the trackKey. Since sourcePath is already resolved and readable (resolveFilePath already called access()), this is a straightforward async swap. The stat call on a network mount (NFS, SMB) can block for 50-500ms, freezing the entire event loop for all concurrent requests during that window.

findings.title: statSync in serveFile also blocks event loop
findings.suggested_fix: In `serveFile()`, replace `statSync(filePath)` with `await stat(filePath)` and make `serveFile` async. This is called for every HLS segment response. For segment files on the local HLS cache dir (usually local disk), this is lower risk than the source statSync, but still wrong form. Callers in `stream.ts` already await, so the async propagation is straightforward.

findings.title: Lidarr metadata caching is present but was not documented as such — no issue with caching itself; however, stat result used to compute trackKey is NOT cached, causing redundant fs.stat per segment
findings.suggested_fix: Cache the computed trackKey per (sourcePath, bitrate) in an in-process TTL map (e.g. 5 minutes) after the first stat. The trackKey is a hash of path+mtime+size+bitrate — all values that change only when a file is replaced by Lidarr. For the common streaming path (same track, many segments), this eliminates one async stat per segment after the first. Store `Map<string, { trackKey: string, expiresAt: number }>` keyed by `${sourcePath}:${bitrate}`. On cache hit, skip the stat entirely. TTL of 5 minutes matches the existing Lidarr metadata cache TTL.

findings.title: No cap on concurrent ffmpeg transcoding processes
findings.suggested_fix: Add a global semaphore (e.g. a simple counter + queue) capping concurrent ffmpeg invocations. Under heavy load with N users each streaming a different track, segment 0 for each track fires simultaneously — N concurrent ffmpeg processes, each decoding audio. A reasonable default cap is 4-8 concurrent transcodes (configurable via env `HLS_MAX_CONCURRENT_TRANSCODES`). Queue excess requests behind the semaphore rather than rejecting them, since the in-flight dedup map already handles concurrent requests for the same segment. Without a cap, a burst of 20 users each starting a new track can spike CPU to 100% and degrade all streams.

findings.title: Cache eviction uses directory mtime which stops updating after all segments are generated
findings.suggested_fix: Directory mtime only changes when files inside are created/deleted. Once all segments for a track are cached, no further writes occur — the directory mtime freezes. A heavily-cached track not played in 24h will be evicted and must be re-transcoded from scratch on next play, even if it was played 23h ago. Consider using a separate sidecar file (e.g. `.last_access`) updated via `utimes()` on each segment read, or an in-process LRU map that persists last-access timestamps. Alternatively, accept this trade-off and document it — for a self-hosted single-user scenario the impact is low, but for multi-user it can cause unnecessary cache churn for popular tracks.

findings.title: Hourly cache cleanup uses all-synchronous fs calls, blocking event loop for large caches
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `readdirSync`, `statSync`, `unlinkSync`, `rmdirSync` in `startHlsCacheCleanup` with their `fs/promises` async equivalents (`readdir`, `stat`, `unlink`, `rmdir`). The cleanup runs every hour via `setInterval` — since it's not on the hot path, impact is low in normal operation. However, if the cache dir contains hundreds of subdirectories (many cached tracks), the synchronous loop can block the event loop for tens of milliseconds per cleanup tick. Convert the body of the `setInterval` callback to an async function.

findings.title: existsSync in ensureSegment hot path — minor per-request fs syscall overhead
findings.suggested_fix: For the warm cache path (segment already generated), `existsSync` is called on every request. On local disk this is ~0.1ms. On a network-mounted HLS cache dir this could be higher. Consider maintaining an in-memory Set of known-good segment keys (`${trackKey}/segment${segmentIndex}`) populated when `generateSegment` resolves and cleared during cache cleanup. This makes cache hits O(1) in memory with zero syscall overhead. The in-flight dedup map already exists, so extending the hot-path Set is minimal additional complexity. Note: after server restart the Set is empty, so the first request per segment still uses existsSync — this is acceptable.

findings.title: Playlist rebuild is O(segmentCount) string ops on every request — low impact now, worth noting
findings.suggested_fix: The playlist is rebuilt from scratch on every request because the token is embedded in every segment URL. For typical tracks (40-100 segments) this is O(microseconds) of string allocation — not worth fixing now. If segment counts grow to 1000+ (very long tracks/podcasts at small segment sizes), consider caching the token-independent parts (the EXTINF duration lines) and appending the token per-request. This is a future optimization trigger, not an immediate action.

findings.title: Metadata cache eviction policy is insertion-order LRU approximation — may evict hot entries under load
findings.suggested_fix: When the cache hits 500 entries, `metaCacheSet` deletes the first key (insertion order), not the least recently used. This is a crude FIFO, not LRU. Under a workload with 500+ unique tracks being streamed, hot tracks inserted early will be evicted while cold tracks inserted recently stay resident. The impact is extra Lidarr API calls for popular tracks. A proper LRU (e.g. using `Map` with delete-and-reinsert on access) would improve cache effectiveness. Low priority for the typical self-hosted scale (500 tracks is a large library to stream simultaneously).

residual_risks: The Lidarr metadata cache (TTL 5 min, max 500 entries) correctly deduplicates getTrack/getTrackFile calls within a session. However, if Lidarr re-downloads a track at higher quality (path changes or mtime changes), the stale cached path will cause the HLS service to transcode from the old file for up to 5 minutes. This is a correctness/freshness tradeoff, not a performance risk, but worth noting.

The `inFlightSegments` map deduplicates concurrent ffmpeg invocations for the same segment. However, the map key includes only trackKey and segmentIndex — not bitrate. Since trackKey already incorporates bitrate (via computeTrackKey), this is correct. No issue, but the key construction should be verified if the key schema ever changes.

The `METADATA_CACHE_MAX = 500` cap is applied per Node.js process. In a clustered deployment (multiple PM2 instances), each process has its own cache, so Lidarr will see N times the API calls under load. The current architecture appears single-process, but this should be documented if horizontal scaling is ever considered.

The `serveFile` function uses synchronous `statSync` even for HLS segment files (local cache). This is called on the response path — after transcoding completes — so it doubles the synchronous fs calls in the hot path of segment serving.

There is no rate limiting on the segment endpoint. A single client with a fast connection could request all N segments in parallel, spawning N concurrent ffmpeg processes for the same track. The in-flight dedup map only deduplicates identical (trackKey, segmentIndex) pairs — requesting all different segments simultaneously bypasses dedup entirely.
testing_gaps: No integration test verifies that statSync is not called on a slow/network filesystem — the test suite mocks fluent-ffmpeg but does not simulate delayed fs.stat responses to detect event loop blocking.

No test asserts that concurrent segment requests for different indices of the same track do not spawn duplicate ffmpeg processes for overlapping segments — the dedup test should verify Map size during concurrent calls.

No load/concurrency test validates behavior when >10 users stream simultaneously. The test suite uses direct processOnePendingDownload calls but has no parallel HTTP request simulation for the segment endpoint.
Cache eviction correctness is not tested: no test verifies that a segment dir with mtime >24h is cleaned up while a recently-accessed dir is preserved.

The trackKey collision scenario (two different source files with the same mtime+size+path) is not tested. While cryptographically improbable, a regression test for computeTrackKey determinism with varied inputs would increase confidence.
No test validates that the `existsSync` fast-path correctly returns the cached segment path on second request without re-invoking generateSegment.
The FIFO eviction behavior of metadataCache at the 500-entry limit is not tested — a test with 501 entries should verify the evicted entry is the first-inserted, not the least-recently-used.
